content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 9 – “P” for PricePart 3 - Price-adjustment strategies
Chapter 9 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

9.4 Explain how companies determine a set of prices that maximises the profits from the total product mix.
9.5 Discuss how companies adjust their prices to take into account different types of customers and situations.
9.6 Discuss the key issues related to initiating and responding to price changes.
Learning Objectives for Lecture 9 for Part 3 (1 of 1)

Part 3  - Price-adjustment strategies

Companies usually adjust their basic prices to account for differences in:
customers
products
locations
changing situations.
Price-adjustment strategies (1 of 9)
Q: Can you think of examples where:
different customers may pay different prices for the same product or service?
a company charges different prices for different locations?

Table 9.2
Price adjustments
Strategy
Description
Discount and allowance pricing
Reducing prices to reward for customer actions such as volume purchases, paying early or promoting the product.
Segmented pricing
Adjusting prices to allow for differences in customers, products or locations.
Psychological pricing
Adjusting prices for psychological effect.
Promotional pricing
Temporarily reducing prices to increase short-term sales
Geographical pricing
Adjusting prices to account for the geographic location of
Customers.
Dynamic pricing
Adjusting prices continually to meet the characteristics and needs of individual customers and situations.
International pricing
Adjusting prices for international markets.

Price-adjustment strategies (3 of 9)
Discount and allowance pricing
A discount is a straight reduction in price on purchase during a stated period of time or when purchasing larger quantities.
Discounts can take many forms, including:
cash discount (for paying in cash), quantity discount (purchasing larger quantities), functional discount (such for acting as a wholesaler or distributor), seasonal discount (to encourage stocking-up before the high selling season starts).
An allowance is promotional monies paid by suppliers to retailers in return for an agreement to feature the suppliers’ products in some way (such as advertising in the catalogue/website, displays in-store).
Allowances include:
trade-in allowances, promotional allowances.

Segmented pricing
Segmented pricing is where a company sells a product or service at two or more prices, even though the difference in price is not based on differences in costs.
Segmented pricing takes many forms, including:
customer-segment pricing – different prices for different segments such as professional compared with (c/w) amateur/consumer, industrial customers c/w end-consumers.
product-form pricing
location-based pricing – different prices because of degree of competition in the local market. For example, supermarkets have higher prices in areas of less competition and lower prices where competition is strong.
time-based pricing. For example, lower prices in the low season c/w high season, dining early in the evening (say “out by 7:45pm”) c/w prime time (say “8pm onwards”).
Price-adjustment strategies (4 of 9)

Psychological pricing
Psychological pricing is where sellers consider the psychology of prices and not simply the economics. (For example, “less than $10, or $20”.)
Reference prices are prices that buyers carry in their minds and refer to when looking at a given product. For example, the price of the leading brand (say Nescafe or Toyota Corolla) , or the brand they consider the best (say Moccona or Mercedes-Benz).
Price-adjustment strategies (5 of 9)
Q: Price says something about the product – or does it? What do you think?

Promotional pricing
Promotional pricing is where a company temporarily prices its product below list price, and sometimes even below cost, to create buying excitement and urgency. And therefore, increase sales in the short term.
Promotional pricing can take many forms, e.g. discounts, rebates, free maintenance, among others.
Price-adjustment strategies (6 of 9)
Q: From the marketer’s perspective, what are some of the disadvantages of promotional prices?

Geographical pricing
Geographical pricing refers to the process where a company sets prices for customers located in different parts of the country or world.
Geographical pricing can take many forms, including:
free on board (FOB)-origin pricing. Price includes delivery to the point or origin e.g. delivery to the freight forwarders in Melbourne, but the buyer then pays the cost of freight from there onwards.
Free in store (FIS). Price includes freight charges to the customer’s “store” i.e. the customer’s delivery point.
uniform-delivered pricing. Price is the same to all customers regardless of their location.
zone pricing. Different prices for customers located in different “zones”. For example:  Zone 1 - Melb Metro, Zone 2 - country Vic, Zone 3 - all other locations.
basing-point pricing. Prices are all “plus freight from base point”. For example, “all prices are plus freight from Melbourne distribution centre” even if the delivery is made from the Sydney distribution centre.
freight-absorption pricing. Therefore, all published prices include fright charges.
Price-adjustment strategies (7 of 9)

Dynamic and personalised pricing
Dynamic pricing refers to the process where prices are adjusted continually to meet the characteristics and needs of individual customers and situations.
Dynamic pricing offers both advantages for marketers and benefits for consumers.
It can also be controversial.
Price-adjustment strategies (8 of 9)
Q: Can you think of situations where dynamic pricing might negatively impact a marketer?

International pricing
Companies that market their products internationally must decide what prices to charge in the different countries in which they operate.
They can either:
set a uniform worldwide price, or
adjust prices to reflect local market conditions and cost considerations.
Factors affecting international pricing include:
economic conditions, competitive situations, consumer perceptions.
Price-adjustment strategies (9 of 9)
Q: What other factors might affect international pricing?

Initiating price changes
When initiating price changes, whether cuts or increases, marketers must consider both:
buyer reactions – for example, will they buy less or more, will they switch to another brand?
competitor reactions – will they lower or raise their prices too?
Responding to price changes
When responding to a price change by a competitor, a firm needs to consider several issues, including:
external factors
its own situation and strategy.
Price changes (1 of 2)

Figure 9.5
Assessing and responding to competitor price changes

Price-adjustment strategies
A:
Different prices for different segments such as professional compared with (c/w) amateur/consumer, industrial customers c/w end-consumers.
Different prices because of degree of competition in the local market. For example, supermarkets have higher prices in areas of less competition and lower prices where competition is strong. Different prices due to different policies for charging of freight costs.
Q: Can you think of examples where:
different customers may pay different prices for the same product or service?
a company charges different prices for different locations?

Price-adjustment strategies
A:
Customers hold-off buying while they wait for the next promotion.
Customers grow to expect a lower price every time they buy.
Customers might come to see the brand as moving down-market/being of lesser quality or position.
Competitors might lower their prices and that might mean that we must keep our prices lower permanently, and
This might lead to price war and/or lower profits for our company and competitors.
Q: From the marketer’s perspective, what are some of the disadvantages of promotional prices?

Price-adjustment strategies
A:
Customers might become confused about the “real” or list price.
They might demand the lowest price every time they buy.
Other customers will learn about their competitors being charged lower prices that they are paying, and then demand that lower price for themselves.
Q: Can you think of situations where dynamic pricing might negatively impact a marketer?

Price-adjustment strategies
A:
The buying power of the local buyers. What can they afford?
The ability of local retailers and wholesalers to control local pricing in-market and therefore charge a high margin and restrict sales of our product.
If we set the price low in an export market, arbitragers might buy at low prices in that market and sell in other markets at the high price that prevails in that other market/s, such as our own “home market”. Termed “grey marketing”.
Q: What other factors might affect international pricing?

Coming up next
